title: Kayson Garn’s CV
text:
BYU-Idaho Student studying Economics, Data Science & Mathematics.


Housing and Student Living Office | Lead Office Assistant | Rexburg, Idaho | April 2024 - Present
Housing department for Brigham Young University of Idaho

Analyzed 890+ student housing survey responses using Python in Visual Studio Code, delivering insights on a quarterly basis

Prepared data-driven reports and presentations using R on occupancy, pricing, and bed counts across 89 apartment complexes (2022–2025)

Communicated regularly with 60+ property managers to address student housing needs and maintain accurate housing data

Audited occupancy, pricing, and contact information across multiple properties to ensure data accuracy and operational efficiency

Econ Games | Team Analyst | Rexburg, Idaho | January 2026 - April 2026

Developed a supply chain model in R that increased projected profits for Brad Hall Fuel from $1,000 to $37,000 per truck annually

Competed in the 2026 Econ Games, analyzing traditional and GPS horse racing data from Equibase within a 24-hour case competition

Presented analytical findings and strategic recommendations as part of a competitive university team



Programs: Python, R, R Markdown, SQL, Git & GitHub, Excel, Microsoft Office
Data & Analytics: Data Wrangling, Data Visualization, Linear Regression Modeling
Management: Analytical Thinking, Financial Management, Adaptability, Project Management, Communication, Public Speaking, Problem-Solving, Conflict Resolution
Languages: Fluent Portuguese, Basic Spanish



Energy Markets, Nuclear Energy, Critical Minerals and Natural Resources,
Housing Markets, Industrial Organization




Bachelor of Science in Economics | March 2024 - July 2027
Brigham Young University - Idaho | 3.99 GPA
Minor: Data Science, Mathematics
Relevant Coursework: Microeconomics, Macroeconomics, Econometrics,
Statistics for Economists, Calculus II, Linear Algebra, Data Science
Programming, Data Wrangling & Visualization



Econ Society | Officer | Rexburg, Idaho | August 2025 - Present

Planned weekly meetings with other econ society officers including networking events, projects, and alumni presentations
Led a housing market analysis project using data from 5 sources, applying Python and R to clean and analyze data for the BYU-Idaho Housing Office

Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints | Assistant to the President / Financial Secretary | Vitória, Brazil | June 2022 - March 2024

Led and trained 110+ volunteers through goal setting, accountability systems, and weekly performance meetings

Managed and audited organizational finances using Excel and Power BI, ensuring alignment with budget goals

Coordinated assignments across 30+ meetings and organized 8 large-scale organizational transfers





Eagle Scout Award, Greenville, North Carolina (2019)

National Honor Society, West Chicago, Illinois (2020)




https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7353528492719841280/
An econometrics research project exploring the relationship between Child Tax Credits and Juvenile Delinquency on the county level across the United States.

title: About this Portfolio
text:
What Does This Portfolio Contain
This portfolio is a representation of academic final projects, research, personal and professional projects and analysis demonstrating skills learned during my time studying Economics, Mathematics, and Data Science.
It explores topics in:
Econometrics: Child Tax Credit and Juvenile Delinquency Relationship
Statistics: Kruskal Wallace (Non-parametric anova test): Electricity Generation Capacity by production type
Economics & Data Science: Student Housing Analysis of Occupancy, Pricing, Bed Count, and Survey Data Brad Hall Fuel Supply Chain Analysis Horse Racing Traditional and GPS Data Demand Analysis

title: Consulting Project- Power Plants
text:
Paste in a template


Show the code
library(mosaic)
library(DT)
library(pander)
library(car)
library(tidyverse)




Show the code
nuclear_data <- read.csv("C:\\Users\\kayso\\OneDrive\\Desktop\\BYUI\\Semesters\\Fall 2025\\Intermediate Stats\\Statistics-Notebook-master\\Statistics-Notebook-master\\Data\\power_plant_database_global.csv")




Show the code
nuclear_data_lim <- select(nuclear_data, c(country_long, capacity_mw, primary_fuel))




Show the code
final_nuc_sources <- filter(nuclear_data_lim, primary_fuel %in% c('Hydro','Nuclear','Solar','Wind', 'Geothermal') )




Show the code
final_nuc_sources <- filter(final_nuc_sources, capacity_mw < 11000)




Results and Executive Summary
The question of whether a country focused on increasing it’s national electricity production capacity, should choose wind, solar, geothermal, hydro, or nuclear electricity is important to answer. Especially for developing economies seeking to increase it’s energy output while remaining ecologically focused.
To answer the question we will look at a combination of data analysis and statistical tests to discover which fuel type is best for the combination of goals listed above. While not exhaustive, this study seeks to make use of a particular data set produced by the World Resources Institute produced in 2021 called the Global Power Plant Data Base.
Based on the data and the application of the Kruskal Wallis test, we can conclude that nuclear, as a fuel source, has a higher average energy production capacity of 2092 mega-watts. The fuel type with the highest outliers is hydro with three observations above that of the highest capacity recorded for the nuclear fuel type.
This observation is supported by the results of a Kruskal-Wallis test that resulted in us concluding that at least one of the fuel types tested had a mean stochastically different from the rest. The graphical and numerical summaries further supported that observation. Therefore, a country seeking for reliable, high capacity, and clean energy sources should seek to invest in nuclear technology and in nuclear reactor construction.
An important note is that hydro plants have higher potential generation capacities, as demonstrated by China with the top three capacity power plants being hydro plants. As such if the developing country has the natural resources available to it, namely strong current rivers and waterfalls, then hydro may be considered a viable clean energy source to pursue the given goals above.


Analysis Process

The Data
Reliable, environmentally sustainable, and affordable energy sector is essential to modern society. Governments, utilities companies, as well as influential companies regularly make decisions that affect the energy sector as well as depend on it.
The Global Power Plant Database is an open-source open-access data set of grid scale (1 MW and greater) electricity generating facilities operating across the world.
The database currently contains nearly 35000 power plants in 167 countries, representing about roughly 72% of the world’s capacity according to the World Resources Institute (2021).
The methodology for the dataset creation is given in the World Resources Institute publication “A Global Database of Power Plants”.
The original data set contains the following fields along with their descriptions:

country (text): 3 character country code corresponding to the ISO 3166-1 alpha-3 specification [5]
country_long (text): longer form of the country designation
name (text): name or title of the power plant, generally in Romanized form
gppd_idnr (text): 10 or 12 character identifier for the power plant
capacity_mw (number): electrical generating capacity in megawatts
latitude (number): geolocation in decimal degrees; WGS84 (EPSG:4326)
longitude (number): geolocation in decimal degrees; WGS84 (EPSG:4326)
primary_fuel (text): energy source used in primary electricity generation or export
other_fuel1 (text): energy source used in electricity generation or export
other_fuel2 (text): energy source used in electricity generation or export
other_fuel3 (text): energy source used in electricity generation or export
commissioning_year (number): year of plant operation, weighted by unit-capacity when data is available
owner (text): majority shareholder of the power plant, generally in Romanized form
source (text): entity reporting the data; could be an organization, report, or document, generally in Romanized form
url (text): web document corresponding to the source field
geolocation_source (text): attribution for geolocation information
wepp_id (text): a reference to a unique plant identifier in the widely-used PLATTS-WEPP database.
year_of_capacity_data (number): year the capacity information was reported
generation_gwh_2013 (number): electricity generation in gigawatt-hours reported for the year 2013
generation_gwh_2014 (number): electricity generation in gigawatt-hours reported for the year 2014
generation_gwh_2015 (number): electricity generation in gigawatt-hours reported for the year 2015
generation_gwh_2016 (number): electricity generation in gigawatt-hours reported for the year 2016
generation_gwh_2017 (number): electricity generation in gigawatt-hours reported for the year 2017
generation_gwh_2018 (number): electricity generation in gigawatt-hours reported for the year 2018
generation_gwh_2019 (number): electricity generation in gigawatt-hours reported for the year 2019
generation_data_source (text): attribution for the reported generation information
estimated_generation_gwh_2013 (number): estimated electricity generation in gigawatt-hours for the year 2013 (see [2])
estimated_generation_gwh_2014 (number): estimated electricity generation in gigawatt-hours for the year 2014 (see [2])
estimated_generation_gwh_2015 (number): estimated electricity generation in gigawatt-hours for the year 2015 (see [2])
estimated_generation_gwh_2016 (number): estimated electricity generation in gigawatt-hours for the year 2016 (see [2])
estimated_generation_gwh_2017 (number): estimated electricity generation in gigawatt-hours for the year 2017 (see [2])
estimated_generation_note_2013 (text): label of the model/method used to estimate generation for the year 2013 (see section on this field below)
estimated_generation_note_2014 (text): label of the model/method used to estimate generation for the year 2014 (see section on this field below)
estimated_generation_note_2015 (text): label of the model/method used to estimate generation for the year 2015 (see section on this field below)
estimated_generation_note_2016 (text): label of the model/method used to estimate generation for the year 2016 (see section on this field below)
estimated_generation_note_2017 (text): label of the model/method used to estimate generation for the year 2017 (see section on this field below)

The citation for the source of the data can be found below:
Title: Global Power Plant Database
Description: A comprehensive, global, open source database of power plants
Version: 1.3.0
Release Date: 2021-06-02
URI: http://datasets.wri.org/dataset/globalpowerplantdatabase
Copyright: Copyright 2018-2021 World Resources Institute and Data Contributors
License: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International – CC BY 4.0
Contact: powerexplorer@wri.org
Citation: Global Energy Observatory, Google, KTH Royal Institute of Technology in Stockholm, Enipedia, World Resources Institute. 2019. Global Power Plant Database. Published on Resource Watch and Google Earth Engine. http://resourcewatch.org/ https://earthengine.google.com/


Data Wrangling Process
First, the data was filtered down to just the three variables country_long, capacity_mw, and primary primary_fuel. Primary fuel type and capacity in mega watts are our numeric and categorical variables of interest while the country name is included for reference.
Second, the data was filtered from fifteen original energy production sources down to five for application of the Anova and Kruskal Wallis tests.


Show the code
pander(table(nuclear_data_lim$primary_fuel))



Table continues below












Biomass
Coal
Cogeneration
Gas
Geothermal
Hydro

Show the code
pander(table(final_nuc_sources$primary_fuel))












Geothermal
Hydro

Third, after visualizing the data and discovering an outlier, that of the Three Gorges Dam in China with a mega watt capacity of 22500, the outlier was removed.
It is interesting to not that the top 3 mega watt capacity energy producers are located in China. The fuel type for all three are hydroelectric.
The above filtering was the only data wrangling applied to this project.


Show the code
datatable(final_nuc_sources[1:100, ])






Only the first 100 results have been included due to the sheer size of the dataset being used in this analysis.


Boxplot Chart and Numeric Summary


Show the code
boxplot(capacity_mw ~ as.factor(primary_fuel), 
        col="lightgray", xlab="Fuel Type (Clean)", main=" World Clean Electricity Production (2021)", ylab="Electrical Capacity M(W)",data=final_nuc_sources, outline = FALSE)









The graph above depicts a box plot for each of the selected electricity generation source types. As can be clearly seen above the box plot for the nuclear source data is different than the rest of the source types. For the purpose of the ANOVA and Kruskal Wallis tests the mean of the nuclear source appears to be different than the rest of the source types. Note that an outlier of the hydro source data was removed to better visualize the data, it has a generation capacity of over 1100 mega watts.
With that outlier in mind, it is also important to note that in addition to nuclear as a energy genration choice for a higher mean, hydro also has a large amount of variation. Thus when determining whether to built a nuclear reactor or a hydro electricity generator it is important to determine the purpose and the style and design of the generator and compare from there.


Show the code
pander(favstats(capacity_mw ~ as.factor(primary_fuel), data=final_nuc_sources))



Table continues below

Notice in the data set that Geothermal and Nuclear have the fewest number of observations. Also, Hydro has the highest max generation capacity while Nuclear has the highest average capacity of energy generation.


Hypotheses
Our null hypothesis for the following Kruskal-Wallis test is that all fuel types have an equal mean mega-watt energy capacity or that the data attributed to each of the fuel types are samples from the same distribution.
   H_0: \text{All samples are from the same distribution.} 
Our alternative hypothesis for the Kruskall-Wallis test is that at least one of the fuel types is a sample from a different distribution from the rest.
   H_a: \text{At least one sample's distribution is stochastically different.}  In the Kruskall-Wallis test we will be using an \alpha= 0.10 to determine the reusts of our hypothesis testing.


Kruskal-Wallis
The Kruskal-Wallis test is an alternative to the Anova test when normality cannot be assumed. As will be explained in the “Diagnostic Plot” section we will use the Kruskall-Wallis test as it appears more appropriate to use given the data presented.
Below is a summary of the Anova test.


Show the code
#to show the anova test output
aovenergy <- aov(capacity_mw ~ as.factor(primary_fuel), data=final_nuc_sources)

(summary(aovenergy))


                           Df    Sum Sq   Mean Sq F value Pr(>F)    
as.factor(primary_fuel)     4 8.620e+08 215499963    2895 <2e-16 ***
Residuals               23541 1.752e+09     74441                   
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1


Below is a summary of the Kruskal-Wallis test.


Show the code
kruskout <- kruskal.test(capacity_mw ~ as.factor(primary_fuel), data=final_nuc_sources)

(kruskout)



    Kruskal-Wallis rank sum test

data:  capacity_mw by as.factor(primary_fuel)
Kruskal-Wallis chi-squared = 3798.5, df = 4, p-value < 2.2e-16


Both tests result in a p-value of less than \alpha=0.05 meaning that we can reject the null hypothesis that the fuel types represented in our data are from the same distribution.


Diagnostic Plots


Show the code
par(mfrow=c(1,2))
plot(aovenergy,which=1:2)









The graph on the left demonstrates that the variances in mega-watt capacities of the various fuel types are different than one another and leads us to conclude that using a Kruskal-Wallis test would be preferable to that of an Anova test.
The graph on the right is depicted to determine whether the data gathered is normally distributed. In this case it is reasonable to conclude that it is not safe to assume normality of the residuals. As such we can conclude that since the assumptions of equal variance and normality are not met for the Anova test is is safer to use the alternative Kruskal-Wallis test for increased accuracy in our conclusions.

title: Client Report - Further Housing Data Analysis
text:
Further Housing Analysis


Show the code
#to read in the data
HSL_data <- rio::import("HSL_data.csv")




Show the code
#to investigate what bins should be created...
HSL_data <- HSL_data |> 
  mutate(property_size  = case_when(
    Total_Beds < 20 ~ "House",
    Total_Beds >=20 & Total_Beds <80 ~ "Small",
    Total_Beds >=80 & Total_Beds <=180 ~ "Medium",
    Total_Beds >180 ~ "Large"
  ))


hist_bins <- ggplot(HSL_data, aes(x=as.factor(property_size))) + geom_bar() + theme_minimal()

hist_bins









So we define the following
House: x < 20
Small: 20 <= x < 80
Medium: 80 <= x <= 180
Large: x > 180
Lets Look at the differences in prices compared to the different sized apartment complexes.

Original Price Comparison


Show the code
HSL_years_high_occ_grouped <- HSL_data |> 
  filter(!is.na(Cleanliness)) |> 
  group_by(Semester, Year, Full_Occ) |> 
  summarise(
    mean_price = mean(Mean_Contract_Price),
    median_occ = median(Occ_Rate) * 100,
    mean_beds = mean(Total_Beds),
    mean_management = mean(Management),
    mean_maintenance = mean(Maintenance_Response),
    mean_cleanliness = mean(Cleanliness),
    mean_activities = mean(Activities_Events),
    mean_culture = mean(Culture),
    mean_value = mean(Value),
    mean_overall = mean(Overall)
  ) |> 
  mutate(
    Semester = case_when(
      Semester == "SP" ~ "Spring",
      Semester == "WI" ~ "Winter"
    )
  )




Show the code
price_chart <- ggplot(HSL_years_high_occ_grouped, 
                      aes(x = Year, y = mean_price, color = Full_Occ)) + 
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) + 
  facet_wrap(~Semester) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c(
      "TRUE" = "#006EB6",
      "FALSE" = "#949598" 
    )
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    panel.spacing = unit(1.5, "lines")
  ) + 
  labs(
    title = "Student Approved Housing 2022-2025",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Mean Price",
    color = "Full Occupancy"
  )

ggplotly(price_chart)








Price Comparison by Property Size


Show the code
HSL_size_spring <- HSL_data |> 
  filter(!is.na(Cleanliness)) |> 
  group_by(Semester, Year, Full_Occ, property_size) |> 
  summarise(
    mean_price = mean(Mean_Contract_Price),
    median_occ = median(Occ_Rate) * 100,
    mean_beds = mean(Total_Beds),
    mean_management = mean(Management),
    mean_maintenance = mean(Maintenance_Response),
    mean_cleanliness = mean(Cleanliness),
    mean_activities = mean(Activities_Events),
    mean_culture = mean(Culture),
    mean_value = mean(Value),
    mean_overall = mean(Overall)
  ) |> 
  mutate(
    Semester = case_when(
      Semester == "SP" ~ "Spring",
      Semester == "WI" ~ "Winter"
    ) 
  ) |>
    filter(Semester == "Spring")




Show the code
price_chart_spring <- ggplot(HSL_size_spring, 
                      aes(x = Year, y = mean_price, color = Full_Occ)) + 
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) + 
  facet_wrap(~property_size) + 
  theme_minimal() + 
  labs(
    y= "Mean Price",
    x=" ",
    title="Spring Price Comparison"
  ) + 
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

price_chart_spring











Show the code
HSL_size_winter <- HSL_data |> 
  filter(!is.na(Cleanliness)) |> 
  group_by(Semester, Year, Full_Occ, property_size) |> 
  summarise(
    mean_price = mean(Mean_Contract_Price),
    median_occ = median(Occ_Rate) * 100,
    mean_beds = mean(Total_Beds),
    mean_management = mean(Management),
    mean_maintenance = mean(Maintenance_Response),
    mean_cleanliness = mean(Cleanliness),
    mean_activities = mean(Activities_Events),
    mean_culture = mean(Culture),
    mean_value = mean(Value),
    mean_overall = mean(Overall)
  ) |> 
  mutate(
    Semester = case_when(
      Semester == "SP" ~ "Spring",
      Semester == "WI" ~ "Winter"
    ) 
  ) |>
    filter(Semester == "Winter")




Show the code
price_chart_winter <- ggplot(HSL_size_winter, 
                      aes(x = Year, y = mean_price, color = Full_Occ)) + 
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) + 
  facet_wrap(~property_size) + 
  theme_minimal() + 
  labs(
    y= "Mean Price",
    x=" ",
    title="Spring Price Comparison"
  ) + 
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

price_chart_winter











Original Occupancy Comparison


Show the code
occ_chart <- ggplot(HSL_years_high_occ_grouped, aes(x=Year, y=median_occ, color= Full_Occ)) + 
  geom_line(size = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) + 
  facet_wrap(~Semester) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c(
      "TRUE" = "#006EB6",
      "FALSE" = "#949598" 
    )) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    panel.spacing = unit(1.5, "lines")
  ) + 
  labs(
    title = "Student Approved Housing 2022-2025",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Mean Occupancy",
    color = "Full Occupancy"
  )

ggplotly(occ_chart)








Occupancy Comparison by Property Size


Show the code
occ_chart_spring <- ggplot(HSL_size_spring, 
                      aes(x = Year, y = median_occ, color = Full_Occ)) + 
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) + 
  facet_wrap(~property_size) + 
  theme_minimal() + 
  labs(
    y= "Mean Occupancy",
    x=" ",
    title="Spring Occupancy Comparison"
  ) + 
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

occ_chart_spring











Show the code
occ_chart_winter <- ggplot(HSL_size_winter, 
                      aes(x = Year, y = median_occ, color = Full_Occ)) + 
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) + 
  facet_wrap(~property_size) + 
  theme_minimal() + 
  labs(
    y= "Mean Occupancy",
    x=" ",
    title="Winter Occupancy Comparison"
  ) + 
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

occ_chart_winter











Original Beds Comparison


Show the code
bedcount_chart <- ggplot(HSL_years_high_occ_grouped, aes(x=Year, y=mean_beds, color= Full_Occ)) + 
  geom_line(size = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) + 
  facet_wrap(~Semester) +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c(
      "TRUE" = "#006EB6",
      "FALSE" = "#949598" 
    )) +
  theme(
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    panel.spacing = unit(1.5, "lines")
  ) + 
  labs(
    title = "Student Approved Housing 2022-2025",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Mean Total Beds",
    color = "Full Occupancy"
  )

ggplotly(bedcount_chart)








Bed Count Comparison by Property Size


Show the code
bed_chart_spring <- ggplot(HSL_size_spring, 
                      aes(x = Year, y = mean_beds, color = Full_Occ)) + 
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) + 
  facet_wrap(~property_size) + 
  theme_minimal() + 
  labs(
    y= "Mean Bed Count",
    x=" ",
    title="Spring Bed Count Comparison"
  ) + 
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

bed_chart_spring











Show the code
bed_chart_winter <- ggplot(HSL_size_winter, 
                      aes(x = Year, y = mean_beds, color = Full_Occ)) + 
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) + 
  facet_wrap(~property_size) + 
  theme_minimal() + 
  labs(
    y= "Mean Bed Count",
    x=" ",
    title="Winter Bed Count Comparison"
  ) + 
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

bed_chart_winter











Show the code
price_chart_spring <- ggplot(HSL_size_spring, 
                      aes(x = Year, y = mean_culture, color = Full_Occ)) + 
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) + 
  facet_wrap(~property_size) + 
  theme_minimal() + 
  labs(
    y= "Survey Rating",
    x=" ",
    title="Spring Culture Comparison"
  ) + 
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

price_chart_spring












Creating the New Variable
Here we want to determine the differences between properties that are full year-round, not-full year-round, full in winter but not spring, and full in spring but not winter.
We will start off by creating that variable titled “Year Occupancy Categories”.


Show the code
#lets start off by pivoting the table wider so that each property has only one row per year:

HSL_wide <- HSL_data |> 
  select(Property, Year, Semester, boolocc) |> 
  pivot_wider(names_from = Semester, values_from = boolocc) |> 
  rename(occ_winter = WI, occ_spring = SP)




Show the code
#now we want to create the actual categorical variables: 

HSL_wide <- HSL_wide |> 
  mutate(Year_Occ_Category = case_when(
    occ_winter==1 & occ_spring == 1 ~"Full Year-Round",
    occ_winter==0 & occ_spring==0 ~"Not Full Year-Round",
    occ_winter==1 & occ_spring==0~ "Full Winter, Not Spring",
    occ_winter==0 & occ_spring==1 ~"Full Spring, Not Winter"
  ))




Show the code
#now I need to join this new variable onto the original dataset 
HSL_data <- HSL_data |> 
  left_join(HSL_wide |> select(Property, Year, Year_Occ_Category),
            by = c("Property","Year"))




Show the code
occ_summary <- HSL_data |>
  group_by(Year_Occ_Category) |>
  summarize(
    avg_occ_rate = mean(Occ_Rate, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_price    = mean(Mean_Contract_Price, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_bedcount    = mean(Total_Beds, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_maintenance  = mean(Maintenance_Response, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_cleanliness  = mean(Cleanliness, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_culture  = mean(Culture, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_properties = n_distinct(Property),
    avg_culture  = mean(Culture, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_activities = mean(Activities_Events, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_management = mean(Management, na.rm = TRUE)
    ) |> 
  filter(!is.na(Year_Occ_Category)) |> 
  filter(Year_Occ_Category != "Full Spring, Not Winter")




Show the code
library(ggplot2)
library(plotly)

# Define consistent category colors
category_colors <- c(
  "Full Year-Round"        = "#006EB6",
  "Not Full Year-Round"    = "#949598",
  "Full Winter, Not Spring" = "#003865",
  "Full Spring, Not Winter" = "#72B5E0"
)


# Helper function to keep styling consistent
make_chart <- function(data, y_var, y_label) {
  p <- ggplot(data, aes(x = Year_Occ_Category, y = .data[[y_var]], fill = Year_Occ_Category)) +
    geom_col() +
    scale_fill_manual(values = category_colors) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(
      plot.title    = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
      axis.text.x   = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1),
      legend.position = "none"
    ) +
    labs(
      title = "Student Approved Housing 2022-2025",
      x     = "Occupancy Category",
      y     = y_label
    )
  ggplotly(p)
}

# Generate each chart
make_chart(occ_summary, "avg_occ_rate",    "Avg Occupancy Rate")






Show the code
make_chart(occ_summary, "avg_price",        "Avg Contract Price")






Show the code
make_chart(occ_summary, "avg_bedcount",     "Avg Total Beds")






Show the code
make_chart(occ_summary, "avg_maintenance",  "Avg Maintenance Response")






Show the code
make_chart(occ_summary, "avg_cleanliness",  "Avg Cleanliness")






Show the code
make_chart(occ_summary, "avg_culture",      "Avg Culture")






Show the code
make_chart(occ_summary, "avg_activities", "Avg Activities")






Show the code
make_chart(occ_summary, "avg_management", "Avg Management")








Show the code
make_chart <- function(data, y_var, y_label) {
  p <- ggplot(data, aes(x = Year_Occ_Category, y = .data[[y_var]], color = Year_Occ_Category, group = 1)) +
    geom_line(size = 1.2, color = "#DDDDDD") +
    geom_point(size = 3) +
    scale_color_manual(values = category_colors) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(
      plot.title      = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
      axis.text.x     = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1),
      legend.position = "none"
    ) +
    labs(
      title = "Student Approved Housing 2022-2025",
      x     = "Occupancy Category",
      y     = y_label
    )
  ggplotly(p)
}

# Generate each chart
make_chart(occ_summary, "avg_occ_rate",    "Average Occupancy Rate")






Show the code
make_chart(occ_summary, "avg_price",        "Average Contract Price")






Show the code
make_chart(occ_summary, "avg_bedcount",     "Average Total Beds")






Show the code
make_chart(occ_summary, "avg_maintenance",  "Average Maintenance Response")






Show the code
make_chart(occ_summary, "avg_cleanliness",  "Average Cleanliness")






Show the code
make_chart(occ_summary, "avg_culture",      "Average Culture")






Show the code
make_chart(occ_summary, "avg_activities",      "Average Activities")






Show the code
make_chart(occ_summary, "avg_management", "Average Management")








Show the code
# List of variables to test
variables <- c("Occ_Rate", "Mean_Contract_Price", "Total_Beds", 
               "Maintenance_Response", "Cleanliness", "Culture", "Activities_Events", " Management")

# Run ANOVA for each variable
anova_results <- map(variables, function(var) {
  formula <- as.formula(paste(var, "~ Year_Occ_Category"))
  aov_result <- aov(formula, data = HSL_data)
  summary(aov_result)[[1]] |> 
    rownames_to_column("Term") |> 
    mutate(Variable = var)
}) |> 
  bind_rows() |> 
  filter(Term == "Year_Occ_Category") |> 
  select(Variable, `F value`, `Pr(>F)`) |> 
  mutate(
    Significant = ifelse(`Pr(>F)` < 0.05, "Yes", "No"),
    `Pr(>F)` = round(`Pr(>F)`, 4),
    `F value` = round(`F value`, 3)
  )

anova_results


     Variable F value Pr(>F) Significant
[1,]        7 139.434 0.0000           2
[2,]        6  12.296 0.0000           2
[3,]        8  10.468 0.0000           2
[4,]        5   4.556 0.0036           2
[5,]        3   4.476 0.0040           2
[6,]        4   7.312 0.0001           2
[7,]        2   1.562 0.1973           1
[8,]        1   2.832 0.0375           2

title: Client Report - Student Approved Housing Data Analysis
text:
Show the code
#loading the occupancy data

occupancy_data <- rio::import("C:\\Users\\kayso\\OneDrive\\Desktop\\BYUI\\Econ Society\\Old Sources\\Clean Part 1\\master_occ_price_management.csv")




Show the code
survey_responses <- rio::import("C:\\Users\\kayso\\OneDrive\\Desktop\\BYUI\\Econ Society\\Old Sources\\Clean Part 1\\master_survey.csv")




BYU–Idaho operates a student-approved housing system designed to support its mission of developing disciple leaders. As part of this model, students must live in approved housing, making the availability, quality, and affordability of these units essential to the student experience. Recent policy adjustments and shifts in admissions trends have raised new questions for current and prospective property developers. This analysis provides a concise overview of the housing landscape in Rexburg, offering data-driven insights to help investors better understand risks, opportunities, and expected market conditions.



To build a comprehensive view of the BYU-Idaho housing landscape, I integrated three distinct data streams: Occupancy Rates (0–100%), Student Surveys (1–5 scale metrics), and Contract Pricing. The primary challenge in this integration was the survey data’s staggered collection schedule, where only one-third of complexes are surveyed each semester.
To ensure a proper analysis despite these gaps, I implemented a multi-stage cleaning and “backfilling” workflow:


I began by joining the occupancy and pricing data with the available survey responses. By filtering for NA values in metrics like Cleanliness, I was able to determine which property-semester combinations lacked survey results.



To resolve the missing data, I created a custom logic function, choose_best_semester. Since student sentiment often carries over from the most recent period, this function prioritized the “best available” match.

The Strategy: If a specific semester (e.g., Winter) was missing survey data, the script searched for and pulled the most relevant data from the adjacent semesters (Fall or Spring) within the same year.
Priority Logic:


Show the code
priority_table <- data.frame(
  Missing_Semester = c("Winter (WI)", "Spring (SP)", "Fall (FA)"),
  Priority_1 = c("Fall (FA)", "Fall (FA)", "Winter (WI)"),
  Priority_2 = c("Spring (SP)", "Winter (WI)", "Spring (SP)")
)

# Display the table
pander(priority_table)










Missing_Semester
Priority_1
Priority_2




Winter (WI)
Fall (FA)
Spring (SP)


Spring (SP)
Fall (FA)
Winter (WI)


Fall (FA)
Winter (WI)
Spring (SP)









Once the “best match” was identified, I used a coalesce operation to merge the alternate survey data into the master set to “fill the holes” while keeping the primary data intact. I then standardized the column names and removed redundant “join-helper” columns to create a clean master file titled HSL_data.



To better categorize property performance, I added a binary occupancy indicator (boolocc). This classifies any complex with an occupancy rate of 95% or higher as a “1” (effectively full), allowing for a better comparison between high-demand properties and those with major vacancies.


Show the code
#now I want to join the price/occupancy and survey results datasets 

full_master1 <- occupancy_data %>%
  left_join(survey_responses, by = c("Semester", "Year", "Property"), keep = TRUE)




Show the code
#what data is missing from the survey?
missing_info <- full_master1 %>%
  filter(is.na(Cleanliness))




Show the code
#to create an aleternate survey dataset to do a second merge to catch the semesters we did not get surveys for (we want the survey semesters to be as close to the real semester for the occupancy and price results as possible.)

survey_alt <- survey_responses %>%
  rename(Survey_Semester = Semester)



missing_filled <- missing_info %>%
  left_join(survey_alt, by = c("Property.x" = "Property", "Year.x"="Year"), suffix = c("", "_alt"))

#we want the many to many relationship so that we can 'choose' the "best" semester.




Show the code
#Choose the best semester by logic

choose_best_semester <- function(survey_sem, desired_sem) {

  prefs <- list(
    WI = c("FA", "SP"),
    SP = c("FA", "WI"),
    FA = c("WI", "SP")
  )

  survey_sem %in% prefs[[desired_sem]]
}


missing_best <- missing_filled %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(is_best = choose_best_semester(Survey_Semester, Semester.x)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  filter(is_best) %>%
  distinct(Property.x, Semester.x, Year.x, .keep_all = TRUE)




Show the code
#keep matched rows from the original join

matched <- full_master1 %>%
  filter(!is.na(Cleanliness))




Show the code
#combined matched + backfilled

final_master <- bind_rows(matched, missing_best) %>%
  select(-is_best)




Show the code
#fix duplicate "_alt" columns

alt_cols <- names(final_master)[str_detect(names(final_master), "_alt$")]

for (alt_col in alt_cols) {

  base_col <- str_remove(alt_col, "_alt")

  final_master[[base_col]] <- coalesce(
    final_master[[base_col]],
    final_master[[alt_col]]
  )
}

final_master <- final_master %>%
  select(-all_of(alt_cols)) %>%
  select(-Survey_Semester)




Show the code
HSL_data <- final_master |> 
  select(Semester.x, Year.x, Property.x, Total_Beds, `#Occ`, Empty_Beds,  Occ_Rate, Mean_Contract_Price, Management_Name, Gender, Management, Maintenance_Response, Cleanliness, Activities_Events, Internet, Culture, Value,  Overall, n_responses) |> 
  rename(Semester = Semester.x, Year= Year.x, Property = Property.x, X.Occ = `#Occ`)









Column Name
Description




Semester
Winter or Spring semesters.


Year
Academic year (2022–2025).


Property
Name of the student-approved housing property.


Total_Beds
Total number of beds at the property.


X.Occ
Number of beds currently occupied.


Empty_Beds
Number of beds unoccupied.


Occ_Rate
Occupancy rate of the property.


Mean_Contract_Price
Mean contract price across all floor plans.


Management_Name
Management company for the property.


Gender
Gender the property is approved for (Men/Women).


Management
Average management survey score (1–5).


Maintenance_Response
Average maintenance response score (1–5).


Cleanliness
Average cleanliness score (1–5).


Activities_Events
Average activities & events score (1–5).


Internet
Average internet satisfaction score (1–5).


Culture
Average culture score (1–5).


Value
Average value rating (1–5).


Overall
Overall satisfaction rating (1–5).









Show the code
#to create an additional columns creating a boolean variable for occupancy 1=completely full, 0= not completely full

HSL_data <- HSL_data |> 
  mutate(Full_Occ = boolocc == 1)
#here instead of boolocc I had OCC_Rate == 1.00 which means that it was 100 percent full instead of the margin of error.

HSL_data <- HSL_data |>
  mutate(
    Management_Name = recode(
      Management_Name,
      "Ball Venture" = "Ball Ventures",
    )
  )

title: Client Report - Student Approved Housing Data Analysis
text:
Findings and Recommendations
An analysis of occupancy trends between 2022 and 2025 reveals an inverse relationship between Winter and Spring semesters. We also see an emerging trend where

The BYU-Idaho Housing market is currently defined by a cyclical demand between semester, in this the gap between Winter and Spring occupancy is widening.
Tier 2 and 3 properties can maintain high-occupancy rates during the Winter and Fall semesters while Tier 1 properties are able to fill during the Spring semester. Thus students are “pushed” to choose Tier 2/3 properties during high occupancy semesters.
Tier 1 properties are characterized by properties with

Spring Semester Prices between $1350 and $1550 a semester
Average Bed Counts of between 50 and 150
Mean Maintenance Survey Ratings of between 3.7 and 4.0
Mean Cleanliness Survey Ratings of between 3.7 and 4.1
Tier 2/3 properties are characterized by properties with
Spring Semester Prices between $1200 and $1350 a semester
Average Bed Counts of between > 150 or < 50 (either large scale apartment complexes or small apartment complexes such as “houses”
Mean Maintenance Survey Ratings of less than 3.75
Mean Cleanliness Survey Ratings of less than 3.75

Apartment complexes with a lower total bed count tend to reach full occupancy while those with higher bed counts do not. The marginal effort to sell each additional contract tends to increase with the number of beds offered by an apartment complex which is a difficulty of maintaining a large apartment complex even though there are benefits of “economies of scale” a higher resident/manager ratio, and more advantageous cleaning/maintenance contracts/apartments.
If the goal is to better the experience of BYU-Idaho students and deliver the best experience what we can see is that students care more around Maintenance and Cleanliness than they do about Culture and Activities on average as evidenced by survey and occupancy results. Also, student approved housing tends to deliver a worse “package overall” during the winter and Fall semesters as evidenced by the fact that when there is low occupancy students tend to remain or move to certain apartment complexes over others. Since high occupancy is close to guaranteed during the Winter and Spring semesters those complexes that are the least competitive are “propped-up” rather than replaced naturally through an open and competitive market.
Recommendations:

To smooth out the cyclical nature of the student approved housing environment implement a mixture of the following:
Calendar Optimization: Change the academic calendar to make spring more advantageous to the average student.
Structural Track Reform: Change to a two track system.
Deregulation of Housing Requirements: Remove the requirement to live in student approve housing, change to a more “free-market” system.
Strict Track Enforcement: Enforce the track system to “smooth” out the semesters.
To improve the student experience:
Encourage the building of high-quality, medium-sized apartment complexes (Tier-1). And reduce the number of low-quality, small-sized apartment complexes/homes (Tier3) in student approved housing.
Reinforce to owners and managers that the apartment complexes that tend to reach full occupancy in the spring tend to have higher maintenance and cleanliness ratings than those that do not fill during the Spring and that Culture and Activities ratings tend to be areas of improvement on an apartment complex that provide less of a “return-on-investment” compared to other “soft-factors” contributing to occupancy.
